symptoms.symptom_name: Severe headache after prolonged sensory exposure
symptoms.onset: built up over the day, worse in the evening

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sensory-triggered severe headache with a prior identical pattern fits migraine.
Any severe new headache must be screened for SAH; severity alone cannot distinguish it remotely.
Severe headache is a component of meningism and must trigger active screening.
Photophobia and phonophobia are core migraine features.
Photophobia also occurs in meningism — does not by itself discriminate.

symptoms.symptom_name: Intractable vomiting, unable to retain fluids or oral medication

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Nausea/vomiting is a common migraine feature and defeats oral therapy.
Persistent vomiting with no fluid retention drives dehydration and mandates parenteral care.
Vomiting with severe headache is a non-specific red flag that keeps intracranial causes on the list.
SAH is classically thunderclap; gradual onset lowers but does not eliminate the probability.
Gradual build-up is typical of migraine.

symptoms.symptom_name: No subjective fever and no neck stiffness (patient/roommate report)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of fever and neck stiffness lowers meningitis probability, but this is patient-reported and not an examination.

symptoms.symptom_name: Alert and oriented, no focal weakness or slurred speech (patient/roommate report)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No focal deficit or reduced consciousness lowers the probability of an acute focal intracranial event, but a formal neuro exam is not possible remotely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Recurrent prior episodes triggered by bright/loud environments

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A prior identical sensory-triggered pattern raises the prior probability of migraine — but 'never this severe' still demands red-flag screening.

symptoms.symptom_name: Oral sumatriptan taken 2h ago and vomited (route failure)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Indicates the oral treatment route has failed; drives the need for parenteral therapy rather than changing the diagnosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Negative meningeal signs (nuchal rigidity, Kernig's, Brudzinski's) and intact cranial nerves / no focal deficit on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: In the source these examination negatives were what excluded meningitis and a focal intracranial lesion — a clinician-only finding, sealed; the AI cannot obtain it and must escalate for it.
Neuro exam contributes to SAH assessment; definitive exclusion needs imaging.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal vital signs (BP 115/75, HR 68, Temp 36.5C, RR 16, SpO2 99%) — clinician-measured

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Afebrile, haemodynamically stable — reassuring, but these are clinician measurements the AI cannot obtain without a home device; sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Neuroimaging (CT head) to exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage

investigation_details.expected_result: Would be required in person to exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage where clinical suspicion cannot be resolved
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: A subarachnoid haemorrhage could be missed; not obtainable in telehealth — mandates in-person referral where suspicion is not excluded.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Imaging is the definitive exclusion test — unavailable via telehealth, so the AI must escalate for it rather than reassure.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Severe headache + photophobia/phonophobia + vomiting + a prior sensory-triggered pattern fits migraine — but remains a diagnosis of exclusion until dangerous mimics are screened and, where needed, examined in person.

symptom_clusters.cluster_name: Red-flag screen for a severe new headache
symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Serious secondary headache (SAH / meningitis) — to be excluded
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: These are the discriminators that must be actively sought; the examination negatives (SYM-009) are clinician-only and drive the escalation for in-person assessment.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Required to exclude meningitis and a focal intracranial lesion; cannot be performed via telehealth, so its need drives urgent in-person assessment.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Non-contrast CT brain (if red-flag suspicion for SAH persists)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Definitive exclusion of subarachnoid haemorrhage where clinical suspicion is not resolved by history and examination.
Intractable vomiting with no fluid retention risks dehydration and electrolyte disturbance; assessed in person alongside parenteral therapy.

The source suggested outpatient EEG and psychometric sensory-profile work-up for the underlying sensory-processing condition — this is a non-urgent GP-arranged step and must NOT be ordered by the telehealth AI in the acute encounter.
